title: Calling Windows GetLastError() Function
text: Calling Windows GetLastError() Function In a 3GL signature used to call Windows API functions, you can define a pseudo-operation with the name ULASTERROR rather than calling GetLastError() . If you use Uniface to activate Windows API functions, and you want to do error handling, you find that activating GetLastError() always returns zero instead of the error from the last activated Windows call. To get the actual error information, in a 3GL signature used to call Windows API functions, you can define a pseudo-operation with the name ULASTERROR , which returns the saved return value of GetLastError() from the previous activate in that same signature. Alternatively, you can give the operation a different name, such as GETLASTERROR , and give it the literal name ULASTERROR (all in uppercase). Note: This is implemented for Windows only. On other platforms, the string ULASTERROR in a signature has no special meaning. Note: Each signature instance has its own copy of the saved error value, and therefore each signature must have its own ULASTERROR pseudo-operation defined. For example, given the following code, the second activate call returns 123 ('Invalid name') as expected. activate "SIG1".SETCURRENTDIRECTORY("@#$%^&*") activate "SIG1".ULASTERROR() Copy However, the following command returns 0 . activate "SIG2".ULASTERROR() Copy Was this topic helpful? Like Dislike
